name: Mathematical Modelling — Real Life into Maths
whatIsIt: MODELLING turns a real situation into maths you can solve. You state your ASSUMPTIONS (what you take as true), choose VARIABLES (what can change), write an EXPRESSION, EQUATION, TABLE or GRAPH, solve it, then VALIDATE (does the answer make sense?) and INTERPRET it back in real words. It's the bridge between everyday problems and the maths that answers them.
whyNeeded: Real problems don't arrive as neat sums — you have to build the maths yourself. Modelling is exactly what engineers, scientists, planners and businesses do: describe the world with maths, solve, and check it against reality.
realLifeUses: Planning a party budget as a cost equation
Predicting travel time from speed and distance
Working out the best-value plan with a formula
Estimating how many buses are needed for a trip

vocabulary.meaning: Something you accept as true to simplify the problem.
The expression, equation, table or graph that describes the situation.
Check the answer makes sense in the real situation.

story.title: The Party Plan
story.text: Aditi plans a party: a fixed ₹200 for decorations plus ₹50 per guest for food. 'How much for 6 guests?' She models it: cost = 200 + 50 × g. With g = 6: 200 + 300 = ₹500. 'Does that seem right?' asks Mummy. Aditi checks — yes, more guests means more cost. 'The model works!'
story.extractedProblem: Decorations cost ₹200 and food is ₹50 per guest. Model the cost and find it for 6 guests.

visual.caption: Real situation → model: cost = 200 (fixed) + 50 × guests. For 6 guests: 200 + 300 = ₹500.

teachingGallery.title: Situation → model → check
teachingGallery.note: Choose variables, build the model, solve, then validate.

examples.caption: A table is a model too.

standardMethod.summary: Understand the situation, state assumptions, choose variables, build a model, solve it, then validate and interpret the answer.
steps: UNDERSTAND the real problem and state ASSUMPTIONS (e.g. price stays fixed).
Choose VARIABLES (what changes) and build a MODEL — expression, equation, table or graph.
VALIDATE (does the answer make sense?) and INTERPRET it back in real-life words.

workedExamples.problem: A taxi charges ₹30 fixed plus ₹10 per km. Model the fare and find it for 5 km.
36 students go on a trip; each bus holds 10. How many buses (model + validate)?
steps: Validate: you can't have 0.6 of a bus — round UP.

commonMistakes.mistake: Giving the raw answer without checking it makes real-life sense (like 3.6 buses).
commonMistakes.fix: Always VALIDATE: does the answer fit reality? Round buses/people up to a whole number, and check the size is sensible.

commonMistakes.mistake: Building the model with the fixed cost multiplied instead of the per-unit part.
commonMistakes.fix: Multiply only the PER-UNIT amount by the variable; add the FIXED amount once. cost = fixed + rate × amount.

easy.q: Model: cost = 100 + 20g. Find the cost for g = 3 (in ₹).
After solving a real problem you should…

medium.q: Taxi: fare = 30 + 10k. Find the fare for k = 4 km (in ₹).
25 students, buses hold 10. How many buses are needed (round up)?

challenge.q: Party: cost = 200 + 50g. Find the cost for 8 guests (in ₹).
A model gives '3.6 buses'. Ravi writes 3 buses. What's the fix?

options.label: Round UP to 4 — everyone needs a seat

challenge.answer: Round UP to 4 — everyone needs a seat

questions.q: Model: cost = 50 + 10n. Find cost for n = 4 (in ₹).
42 students, buses of 10 — how many buses (round up)?
A good model of 'fixed cost + cost per item' is…

teachBackPrompt: Pick a real plan with a grown-up (a party, a trip, or a phone bill). Build a simple model like 'cost = fixed + rate × amount', work out the cost for a few amounts, and CHECK your answers make sense in real life.

revisionCard.summary: Modelling: understand → assumptions → variables → build a model (expression/equation/table/graph) → solve → VALIDATE (does it make sense?) → interpret in real words. Pattern for costs: fixed + rate × amount. Round people/buses UP.
